programs.name: Louisiana Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waivers
programs.tagline: Get Medicaid to pay for home care, adult day care, and respite services so your parent can avoid nursing home placement.

localEntities.name: Office of Aging and Adult Services (CCW): https://ldh.la.gov/office-of-aging-and-adult-services[4]

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or managing medications but wants to stay home instead of moving to a nursing home, Louisiana's HCBS waivers can pay for home care services through Medicaid. Because these waivers cover personal care attendants, adult day health care, and respite care under one program, your parent gets coordinated support without you managing multiple providers.

Your parent must be 21+ and meet the same clinical requirements as nursing home admission (called Nursing Facility Level of Care). Income limit: around $943/month for a single person in 2023, with assets limited to $2,000 individual/$3,000 couple. The program has limited slots with priority given to abuse/neglect cases, ALS patients, and current nursing home residents on Medicaid.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (clinical assessment showing inability to perform daily activities like bathing or dressing independently, unstable medical conditions, or cognitive impairments like dementia)
otherRequirements: At risk of nursing home placement
Not receiving PACE or other HCBS waivers

applicationGuide.summary: Contact the Office of Aging and Adult Services online or call your local Medicaid office, though processing time varies and there's a waitlist.

steps.title: Contact the Office of Aging and Adult Services
steps.description: Visit https://ldh.la.gov/office-of-aging-and-adult-services/community-choices-waiver-ccw or call your local Medicaid office at 1-855-618-2229

steps.title: Get medical evaluation
steps.description: Your parent's physician completes a medical evaluation report documenting need for nursing home level care

steps.title: Complete Level II assessment
steps.description: A support coordinator completes BHSF Form 142 to assess your parent's specific care needs

steps.title: Submit financial documents
steps.description: Provide bank statements, income proof, and other documents to verify Medicaid Long-Term Care eligibility

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified; depends on medical certification and financial eligibility approval
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, limited slots with priority groups getting first access
applicationGuide.tip: Priority goes to abuse/neglect referrals and ALS patients - if your parent has ALS, mention this immediately when applying

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Assets transferred below fair value in past 60 months (Look-Back Rule)
excluded: Primary home where your parent lives

tiers.name: Priority 3: Current Residents
tiers.description: Already in nursing home with Medicaid as sole payer

categories.name: Financial
items: Pay stubs or Social Security statements
Financial documents for Medicaid LTC eligibility

categories.name: Medical
items: Documentation of Activities of Daily Living limitations

contentSections.text: Limited enrollment means even eligible applicants may wait months or years for services. Priority groups get first access, so non-priority applicants face long waitlists.
A dementia diagnosis alone won't qualify your parent - they must demonstrate inability to perform daily activities independently through clinical assessment.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for HCBS waivers?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from the $2,000 asset limit as long as your parent lives there. Only secondary properties count toward the limit.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the $943 limit?
faqs.answer: He may still qualify if medical expenses bring his countable income below the limit through Medicaid spend-down rules. A Medicaid eligibility worker can calculate this.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist for non-priority applicants?
faqs.answer: Louisiana doesn't publish specific wait times, but limited slots mean it could be months to years. Priority goes to abuse cases, ALS patients, and current nursing home residents first.

faqs.question: What happens if we transferred money to family in the past 5 years?
faqs.answer: The 60-month Look-Back Rule applies - transfers below fair market value create penalty periods where your parent can't receive benefits. The penalty length depends on the transfer amount.

faqs.question: Can Dad get HCBS services if he's already getting other help?
faqs.answer: No, HCBS waivers exclude people already receiving PACE, LT-PCS (Long-Term Personal Care Services), or other HCBS waivers. You can't combine these programs.

faqs.question: What specific services will the waiver cover for Mom?
faqs.answer: Services depend on assessed needs but can include personal care attendants, adult day health care, respite care, and support coordination. Hours and specific services vary based on your parent's Level of Care assessment.

programs.name: Louisiana Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: All medical care and daily support under one team if your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home.

localEntities.name: Baton Rouge, Greater New Orleans, Alexandria, Lafayette

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs significant daily help but wants to avoid a nursing home, PACE could provide everything — doctors, medications, home care, meals, transportation — under one coordinated team at no extra cost. Because PACE covers all medical care through one program, your parent won't juggle multiple providers or insurance claims.

Your parent must qualify for nursing home-level care (needing daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management) and meet Medicaid income limits: $2,163/month for individuals, $4,326/month for married couples where both receive services. PACE is only available in select areas around New Orleans, Baton Rouge, Lafayette, and Alexandria — not statewide.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must qualify for Nursing Facility Level of Care (needing daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Live in PACE service area (Greater New Orleans, Baton Rouge, Lafayette, or Alexandria regions)
Able to live safely in community with support
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional PACE office for initial screening — the process involves multiple assessments to determine care needs.

steps.title: Contact regional PACE office
steps.description: Call the office serving your parent's zip code for initial screening and information

steps.title: Complete LOCET screening
steps.description: Initial Level of Care Evaluation Tool assessment to determine potential eligibility

steps.title: Full iHC assessment
steps.description: Comprehensive assessment if LOCET indicates nursing facility level of care needed

steps.title: Medicaid eligibility review
steps.description: Financial documentation review for income and asset limits

steps.title: Plan of care development
steps.description: PACE team creates individualized care plan if approved

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — involves multiple assessment stages
applicationGuide.tip: Gather medical records and financial documents before calling — the assessment process requires detailed health and income information.

offices.name: Baton Rouge PACE
offices.address: East and West Baton Rouge service area

offices.name: Greater New Orleans PACE
offices.address: Select zip codes in Greater New Orleans

offices.name: Lafayette PACE
offices.address: Select zip codes in Lafayette area

categories.name: Identity & Residence
items: Birth certificate or ID showing age 55+
Proof of address in PACE service area

contentSections.text: PACE is not available statewide. Your parent must live in specific zip codes around New Orleans, Baton Rouge, Lafayette, or Alexandria. Call to confirm your address qualifies before starting the application process.
Your parent can try PACE without losing other benefits. PACE enrollment is voluntary and they can disenroll if the program doesn't meet their needs, though this involves reassessment.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home doesn't count toward the $2,000 asset limit for individuals. She can live at home and receive PACE services there.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over $2,163 per month?
faqs.answer: He may still qualify through Medicaid spend-down, where excess income goes toward medical expenses. The general long-term care threshold is higher at $2,901/month. Call to discuss specific options.

faqs.question: Does PACE replace Medicare and other insurance?
faqs.answer: PACE becomes the primary provider for all covered services. Your parent keeps Medicare but cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage. PACE coordinates all care instead of managing multiple insurance plans.

faqs.question: What if my parent needs 24-hour nursing care?
faqs.answer: PACE is for people who can live safely in the community with support services. If your parent needs round-the-clock skilled nursing, they may not qualify for PACE and might need nursing facility care instead.

faqs.question: How quickly can my parent start receiving services?
faqs.answer: The timeline isn't specified and involves multiple assessments. After the initial LOCET screening, there's a comprehensive iHC assessment, Medicaid eligibility review, and plan development. Call your regional office for current processing times.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a different parish?
faqs.answer: PACE is only available in select service areas. If you move outside the coverage area, your parent would need to disenroll and find alternative care arrangements. Check coverage areas before relocating.

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Monthly grocery money for parents on fixed incomes — up to $415/month for couples.

localEntities.name: Louisiana Department of Children & Family Services (DCFS)
Parish Councils on Aging (for application assistance and SFMNP)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggling with grocery costs, SNAP provides monthly benefits to help buy food. Louisiana offers more generous income limits than federal rules — your parent may qualify even if they earn more than the standard limits. Because households with elderly members (60+) only need to meet net income requirements, your parent can qualify with higher gross income than younger adults.

For example, a couple with $1,200 monthly income receives $415/month in SNAP benefits. Your parent can use these benefits at grocery stores, farmers markets, and many online retailers. Louisiana eliminated asset limits for most households, so your parent's savings or home value won't disqualify them.
programs.savingsSource: Based on example benefit amounts for elderly/disabled households

otherRequirements: Must have Social Security number or proof of application

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through DCFS or get help from your parish Council on Aging.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect Social Security statements, pension documentation, proof of residence, and medical expense records if applicable.

steps.title: Choose application method
steps.description: Apply online through Louisiana DCFS website, or contact your parish Council on Aging for in-person assistance.

steps.title: Complete eligibility interview
steps.description: DCFS will schedule a phone or in-person interview to verify information and determine benefit amount.

steps.title: Receive EBT card
steps.description: If approved, your parent will receive a Louisiana Purchase EBT card loaded with monthly benefits.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is 60+ with no earned income, ask about ESAP (Elderly Simplified Application Project) — it provides 36-month certification instead of annual renewals.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits for Elderly/Disabled Households
contentSections.footnote: 2025 Louisiana SNAP income limits for households with members 60+ or disabled

categories.name: Identity
items: Social Security card or proof of application
Proof of U.S. citizenship or lawful presence

categories.name: Financial
items: Medical expense receipts (for net income calculation)

contentSections.text: Louisiana has no asset limits for most households. Your parent won't lose SNAP benefits because of savings in the bank or home ownership — only monthly income matters for qualification.

All income counts toward limits, including Social Security, veterans' benefits, and disability payments — not just wages. However, medical expenses can reduce net income for elderly/disabled households.

faqs.question: What if Mom's Social Security puts her just over the income limit?
faqs.answer: If your mom is 60+ or disabled, medical expenses can reduce her countable income. Deductions for medical costs over $35/month, Medicare premiums, and prescription costs may bring her income below the limit.

faqs.question: Can Dad keep his house and still get SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes. Louisiana doesn't count homes or vehicles as assets for SNAP eligibility. Your dad's house, car, and personal belongings won't affect his qualification — only his monthly income matters.

faqs.question: Does my parent have to reapply every year?
faqs.answer: It depends. Standard SNAP requires periodic recertification. But if your parent is 60+ with no earned income, they may qualify for ESAP, which provides 36-month certification with no recertification interviews required.

faqs.question: What stores accept SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: Most grocery stores, many pharmacies, and some online retailers (including Amazon and Walmart) accept SNAP EBT cards. Your parent cannot use SNAP for alcohol, tobacco, hot prepared foods, or non-food items.

faqs.question: How much will my parent receive each month?
faqs.answer: Benefit amounts depend on household size and income. For example, a two-person elderly household with $1,200 monthly income receives $415/month. Higher income means lower benefits, but even small amounts help with grocery costs.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives with me — does my income count?
faqs.answer: Only if you buy and prepare food together. If your parent shops and cooks separately, your income doesn't count toward their SNAP household. But if you share meals and grocery expenses, you'd apply together as one household.
